name: Prose: "The Tempest" — Tales From Shakespeare

sourceRefs.label: 2025_q1 page 7 Q11 (true statements about the play — NOT written 1500, IS a late play, IS based on a real shipwreck, with answer key)
2024_q page 69 Q117; 2022_q page 8 Q25 (Prospero's daughter=Miranda; Ferdinand/Miranda play Chess, stashed earlier, with answer key)
civilserviceaspirants.in - two dedicated MCQ sets (12 questions total); brainkart.com - full plot summary (Prospero/Ariel/Caliban)

researchNotes: Consumes 2 facts stashed since early in the project (from researching 'His First Flight' and 'Learning the Game', which shared OCR pages with stray Tempest questions) plus 1 fresh real hit and a rich 12-question web set. This is Charles & Mary Lamb's prose RETELLING ('Tales from Shakespeare'), not Shakespeare's original play script — explaining why it's a 'prose' entry in Unit VII rather than verse.

reading.intro: Charles and Mary Lamb's prose retelling of Shakespeare's play: Prospero, the overthrown Duke of Milan, and his daughter Miranda live marooned on an island with the spirit Ariel and the enslaved Caliban — until Prospero conjures a storm to bring his old enemies to the island, setting up reconciliation rather than revenge.

qa.question: Real exam: Is it true that 'The Tempest' was written in 1500? What IS true about when it was written, relative to Shakespeare's career?
qa.answer: NO — 'written in 1500' is FALSE (Shakespeare actually wrote it around 1610-1611). What IS true, confirmed by a real exam: it is considered one of the LAST plays William Shakespeare wrote.

qa.question: Real exam: Is 'The Tempest' believed to be based on a real event?
qa.answer: Yes — confirmed true by a real exam question: it is believed to be based on an ACTUAL shipwreck (commonly identified as the 1609 wreck of the Sea Venture off Bermuda).

qa.question: Where do Prospero and Miranda live, and why did they end up there?
qa.answer: In a cave on an isolated island. They ended up there because Prospero, the rightful Duke of Milan, was EXILED by his brother Antonio, who usurped his dukedom.

qa.question: Who is Ariel, who is Caliban, and what does Prospero use to control the spirits and elements?
qa.answer: Ariel is a spirit FREED by Prospero (from imprisonment by the witch Sycorax) and made obedient to him. Caliban is enslaved by Prospero. Prospero controls the spirits and elements using a magic WAND.

qa.question: Why did Prospero raise the storm (the 'tempest') at sea?
qa.answer: To bring his enemies — the King of Naples and his own brother Antonio — to the island, setting up the story's resolution.

qa.question: Real exam: Who is Prospero's daughter, and what game did she play with Ferdinand on the island?
qa.answer: Miranda is Prospero's daughter — confirmed by a real exam question. She and Ferdinand played CHESS together on the island — also confirmed by a real exam question.

qa.question: What task did Prospero assign to Ferdinand, why, and what was the relationship between Ferdinand and Miranda?
qa.answer: Prospero assigned Ferdinand the hard labour of PILING LOGS OF WOOD — a deliberate test of Ferdinand's love and devotion to Miranda. Miranda and Ferdinand admired each other (the seeds of a romantic relationship).

qa.question: How did Ariel torment the King of Naples and Antonio, and how did they feel afterward?
qa.answer: By creating illusions and strange sounds. Afterward, they felt sorry and repentant — setting up Prospero's eventual forgiveness rather than revenge.

qa.question: What did Prospero do before finally leaving the island?
qa.answer: He forgave everyone (his former enemies) and set Ariel free.

qa.question: Who helped Prospero survive when he was first cast out to sea with infant Miranda?
What does the reconciliation-over-revenge ending suggest about the play's overall moral message?
qa.answer: That forgiveness and reconciliation are more powerful and noble responses to betrayal than revenge — Prospero, despite having complete magical power over his enemies, chooses mercy once they show genuine remorse.

qa.question: Why is this text categorized as 'Prose' in the syllabus despite being based on a Shakespeare play?
qa.answer: Because it's Charles and Mary Lamb's PROSE retelling (from *Tales from Shakespeare*), which converts Shakespeare's original verse play into narrative prose form for younger readers — not the original dramatic script.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is TRUE about the play 'The Tempest'?
options: It is considered one of Shakespeare's last plays
It was Shakespeare's very first play
It has no connection to any real historical event

questions.prompt: 'The Tempest' is believed to be based on:
options: A real war between Naples and Milan

questions.prompt: Where did Prospero and Miranda live?
options: In a cottage in the woods
In a cave on an island
questions.explanation: In a cave on an island.

questions.prompt: Who was Ariel in the story?
Why did Prospero and Miranda end up on the island?
What did Prospero use to control the spirits and the elements?
What task did Prospero assign to Ferdinand as a test of his love for Miranda?
What game did Ferdinand and Miranda play together on the island?
How did Ariel torment the King of Naples and Antonio?
options: By sending them back to sea
By pretending to be a monster
By imprisoning them in a cave
By creating illusions and strange sounds
questions.explanation: By creating illusions and strange sounds.

questions.prompt: What did Prospero do before finally leaving the island?
options: Forgave everyone and set Ariel free
Took over the island as king
questions.explanation: Forgave everyone and set Ariel free.

questions.prompt: This version of "The Tempest" is categorized as 'Prose' in the syllabus because it is:
options: Charles and Mary Lamb's prose retelling from Tales from Shakespeare
questions.explanation: Charles and Mary Lamb's prose retelling.
